HOW DID IT COME TO THIS?

by Marc Moszkowski, Founder and Former CEO of DeepGulf, Inc.

(Cliquer ici pour la version en français)

 

At the age of seventy-three, after a lifetime of work, expatriation, successful engineering projects, and nearly a decade of legal battles spanning three continents, I face being dispossessed of the only home I have ever owned and of all my belongings.

The property now facing judicial sale to the exclusive benefit of others is my ancestral family home. Over the years, I have paid inheritance and property taxes equivalent to €715,000 in present value, representing the entirety of my lifetime savings. The one thousand square meters of my estate contains six thousand books, innumerable family archives, dozens of paintings, antique furniture, personal papers, and the accumulated memories of several generations.

To lose such a place while penniless, without transportation, without family support, without friends, and with no practical possibility of beginning again elsewhere, would not merely be a financial loss, it would represent the final erasure of an entire life's work and history.

What makes the present situation particularly difficult to accept is the disparity between what is at stake and the amount from which the proceedings originated.

Yet the proceedings ultimately originate from a disputed amount of €38,250, derived from a transaction arranged by the same individual who had previously undertaken, in signed documents submitted to the United States Department of Homeland Security, to ensure payment of my salary and personally guaranteed those obligations. The unpaid salaries and related obligations arising from those undertakings exceed one million U.S. dollars before interest.

The disproportion between the frivolity of the claim and the consequences now confronted by a seventy-three-year-old man facing the loss of his home, possessions, and means of existence, raises questions that deserve careful examination.

I see no dignified escape from this absurdity.

The documents collected in this repository are therefore not only a record of litigation. They are also a record of the events that brought about this situation, preserved so that others may examine them and reach their own conclusions.

 

See the complete history at:

https://marc-moszkowski.github.io/MMM/Public.html
